title: Old city walls and modern traffic
summary: Argue whether ancient city walls that slow traffic should be preserved intact or partially modified for modern needs, using present perfect, conditionals, and concession.
topic: Whether old city walls should be preserved even if they block modern traffic
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) arguing whether an old city wall that disrupts modern traffic flow should be preserved completely, partially modified, or removed. Choose a clear position and support it with at least one concrete example. Acknowledge the strongest objection to your view and use present perfect, a modal, and 'although' or 'however' at least once each.
hintStarters: The walls have stood for centuries, and in that time they have become…
Although removing the walls would solve the traffic problem, it would also…
If the city were to partially modify the walls, it might be possible to…

hintVocab.meaningVi: thích nghi với nhu cầu hiện đại
một mất mát không thể bù đắp

hintVocab.phrase: a tangible link to the past
hintVocab.meaningVi: một mối liên hệ hữu hình với quá khứ
làm suy yếu tính toàn vẹn cấu trúc
cân bằng các lợi ích cạnh tranh nhau
điểm thắt cổ chai, điểm tắc nghẽn
đứng vững qua nhiều thế kỷ

sampleText:
The old walls of Dubrovnik have stood for roughly seven centuries, and for the past thirty years they have attracted more visitors than almost any other site in the Adriatic. They have also created one of the most persistent traffic problems in Croatia: the old town's narrow gate is the only vehicle entry point for deliveries, emergency services, and residents, and on summer mornings the queue stretches back half a kilometre. The city has debated, without resolution, whether to cut an additional opening through the wall or whether to find another solution altogether. Both sides have reasonable arguments, but only one position is reversible.

I believe the walls should be preserved without modification, and that the city should instead commit to a different traffic strategy. Dubrovnik has already shown that it can reroute most private vehicles to a car park outside the walls; the remaining congestion is caused by delivery vehicles that have no alternative entry. The solution is not to damage the wall but to build a modern goods-transfer station at the outer car park, where large lorries unload into smaller electric vehicles that can use the existing gate. Several historic cities in Italy — Siena and Lucca among them — have adopted similar systems without touching their medieval fortifications. If Dubrovnik were to cut a new opening, it would not only weaken the structural integrity of a monument that has survived bombardment and earthquakes, but it would also set a precedent that any future inconvenience could justify permanent damage to heritage. That is a threshold the city should be very reluctant to cross, especially when a workable alternative has not yet been seriously tested.

Although the proposal to modify the walls has real practical appeal, it rests on a false assumption: that the wall is primarily a transport obstacle rather than the reason people come at all. The walls generate enormous revenue for the city. Removing or modifying them to improve traffic flow would be solving a short-term problem by destroying the thing that funds the solution. Cities that have taken that trade-off — and there are several across Eastern Europe — have rarely found that the freed traffic flow compensated for the loss in tourism and civic identity. The wall is not the problem; the delivery system is. That is what should be redesigned, and it is the question the council has so far declined to ask.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for actions with ongoing consequences
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The city has debated, without resolution, whether to cut an additional opening…' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành vì cuộc tranh luận bắt đầu trong quá khứ và vẫn chưa được giải quyết — tình trạng không có kết quả vẫn đang tiếp tục đến hiện tại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The city has debated, without resolution, whether to cut an additional opening…' uses the present perfect because the debate began in the past and remains unresolved — the state of no conclusion continues into the present.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional for hypothetical consequences
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If Dubrovnik were to cut a new opening, it would not only weaken the structural integrity…' dùng điều kiện loại 2 với 'were to' (trang trọng hơn 'was') để nói về một tình huống giả thuyết có thể xảy ra nhưng chưa xảy ra.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If Dubrovnik were to cut a new opening, it would not only weaken the structural integrity…' uses a second conditional with 'were to' (more formal than 'was') to discuss a hypothetical scenario that is possible but has not happened.

sampleTranslationVi:
Những bức tường cổ của Dubrovnik đã đứng vững khoảng bảy thế kỷ, và trong ba mươi năm qua, chúng đã thu hút nhiều du khách hơn hầu hết các địa điểm khác ở vùng Adriatic. Chúng cũng tạo ra một trong những vấn đề giao thông dai dẳng nhất ở Croatia: cổng hẹp của khu phố cổ là điểm vào duy nhất cho xe giao hàng, dịch vụ khẩn cấp và cư dân, và vào những buổi sáng mùa hè, hàng xe kéo dài nửa cây số. Thành phố đã tranh luận, nhưng chưa có kết quả, về việc có nên cắt thêm một cổng qua tường hay tìm giải pháp khác hoàn toàn.

Tôi tin rằng những bức tường nên được bảo tồn mà không cần sửa đổi, và thay vào đó thành phố nên cam kết theo đuổi một chiến lược giao thông khác. Dubrovnik đã chứng minh rằng họ có thể chuyển hướng hầu hết các phương tiện cá nhân đến một bãi đậu xe bên ngoài tường; tình trạng tắc nghẽn còn lại là do xe giao hàng không có lối vào thay thế. Giải pháp không phải là phá hỏng tường mà là xây dựng một trạm trung chuyển hàng hóa hiện đại tại bãi đậu xe ngoài, nơi các xe tải lớn dỡ hàng vào các xe điện nhỏ hơn có thể sử dụng cổng hiện có. Một số thành phố lịch sử ở Ý — Siena và Lucca trong số đó — đã áp dụng các hệ thống tương tự mà không động đến các công sự thời trung cổ. Nếu Dubrovnik cắt một cổng mới, điều đó không chỉ làm suy yếu tính toàn vẹn cấu trúc của một công trình đã sống sót qua bom đạn và động đất, mà còn tạo tiền lệ rằng bất kỳ sự bất tiện nào trong tương lai cũng có thể biện minh cho việc phá hoại vĩnh viễn di sản.

Mặc dù đề xuất sửa đổi tường có sức hấp dẫn thực tế thực sự, nhưng nó dựa trên một giả định sai lầm: rằng bức tường chủ yếu là chướng ngại vật giao thông thay vì là lý do người ta đến đây. Những bức tường tạo ra nguồn thu khổng lồ cho thành phố. Việc tháo dỡ hoặc sửa đổi chúng để cải thiện luồng giao thông sẽ là giải quyết vấn đề ngắn hạn bằng cách phá hủy thứ tài trợ cho giải pháp đó. Các thành phố đã thực hiện sự đánh đổi đó — và có một số trường hợp trên khắp Đông Âu — hiếm khi thấy rằng luồng giao thông thông thoáng hơn đã bù đắp cho sự mất mát về du lịch và bản sắc của thành phố. Bức tường không phải là vấn đề; hệ thống giao hàng mới là. Đó là thứ cần được thiết kế lại.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly to describe a long-standing situation?
options: The walls have been standing in this city since seven centuries.
The walls have stood in this city for seven centuries.
The walls stood in this city for seven centuries.
The walls has stood in this city for seven centuries.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect (have + past participle) with 'for' + a duration describes a situation that started in the past and continues to now. 'Have been standing' with 'since seven centuries' combines the wrong preposition. 'Stood' is past simple, suggesting the walls no longer stand. 'Has' is singular — the subject 'walls' is plural.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'have + past participle' + 'for' (not 'since') with a duration of time.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a second conditional correctly to describe a hypothetical consequence?
options: If the city cuts a new opening, it would weaken the wall's structure.
If the city were to cut a new opening, it will weaken the wall's structure.
If the city were to cut a new opening, it would weaken the wall's structure.
If the city would cut a new opening, it would weaken the wall's structure.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional with 'were to': if + were to + infinitive, would + base verb. 'Cuts' is present simple (first conditional). 'Will' is future (first conditional). 'Would cut … would' is non-standard — 'would' does not appear in the if-clause.
mcQuestions.hint: Second conditional = if + past form (or 'were to' + infinitive), main clause with 'would'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly to concede a point?
options: Although the proposal has practical appeal but it rests on a false assumption.
Although, the proposal has practical appeal it rests on a false assumption.
The proposal has practical appeal although it rests on a false assumption.
Although the proposal has practical appeal, it rests on a false assumption.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but' after the comma. Comma immediately after 'although' is wrong. The last option is grammatically possible but reverses the rhetorical direction — the conceded point should come first in an argument.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' concedes a point; the main argument follows after the comma with no 'but'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase naturally describes the idea of redirecting vehicles away from a problem area?
options: The city should reroute delivery vehicles around the old town.
The city should reroutine delivery vehicles around the old town.
The city should redirect delivery vehicles through another way around the old town.
The city should re-route delivery vehicles on another direction around the old town.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Reroute' is the standard verb for redirecting traffic. 'Reroutine' is not a word. 'Redirect … through another way' is awkward and non-standard. 'On another direction' uses the wrong preposition — 'in another direction' would be more natural, but 'reroute' already carries this meaning.
mcQuestions.hint: There is a single, common verb in traffic and transport contexts for redirecting a route.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'not only … but also' to list two negative consequences?
options: It would not only weaken the wall but it would also set a dangerous precedent.
It would not only weaken the wall but also set a dangerous precedent.
It not only would weaken the wall but also set a dangerous precedent.
It would not only weaken the wall but also sets a dangerous precedent.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Would not only + verb but also + verb' — both verbs are bare infinitives parallel after 'would'. Adding 'it would' repeats the subject awkwardly. Fronting 'not only' before 'would' requires subject-auxiliary inversion. 'Sets' breaks parallel structure.
mcQuestions.hint: In 'not only … but also', both verbs should be parallel bare infinitives after the shared modal.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'commit to' to express a strong decision?
options: The city should commit a different delivery strategy.
The city should commit on a different delivery strategy.
The city should commit to a different delivery strategy.
The city should commit for a different delivery strategy.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Commit to' is the correct phrasal verb meaning to decide firmly on a course of action. The preposition is 'to', not omitted, and not 'on' or 'for'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Commit' requires a specific preposition to form the phrasal verb meaning 'to firmly decide'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'among' to mention items within a larger group?
options: Several Italian cities — Siena and Lucca between them — have found alternative solutions.
Several Italian cities — Siena and Lucca among these — have found alternative solutions.
Several Italian cities — Siena and Lucca among which — have found alternative solutions.
Several Italian cities — Siena and Lucca among them — have found alternative solutions.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Among them' is the standard parenthetical phrase when you name members of a previously mentioned group. 'Between them' implies only two items sharing something. 'Among these' is slightly less natural as a dash insert. 'Among which' would require a relative clause construction, not a dash insert.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Among them' is the natural parenthetical when citing examples from a list.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'compensate for' to describe making up for a loss?
options: The freed traffic flow rarely compensated for the loss in tourism.
The freed traffic flow rarely compensated the loss in tourism.
The freed traffic flow rarely compensated about the loss in tourism.
The freed traffic flow rarely compensated with the loss in tourism.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Compensate for' is the correct phrasal verb. Omitting the preposition, or using 'about' or 'with', breaks the standard collocation.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Compensate' requires a specific preposition to mean 'make up for a loss'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a modal to express a recommendation about city planning?
options: The delivery system is what should be redesign, not the walls.
The delivery system is what should be redesigned, not the walls.
The delivery system is what should be redesigning, not the walls.
The delivery system is what should redesign, not the walls.
mcQuestions.explanation: Modal passive = should + be + past participle. 'Should be redesign' uses the base form. 'Should be redesigning' uses the present participle. 'Should redesign' is active, changing the meaning — it implies the system will redesign itself.
mcQuestions.hint: Modal passive = should + be + past participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase most naturally describes a tangible connection to history?
options: The walls are a tangible connection with the past.
The walls are a palpable link for the past.
The walls are a tangible link to the past.
The walls are a touching link towards the past.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A tangible link to the past' is the natural collocation. 'Connection with' is possible but 'link to' is more established in heritage contexts. 'Palpable link for' is not standard. 'Touching' means emotionally moving, and 'towards' implies direction rather than connection.
mcQuestions.hint: Which preposition collocates most naturally with 'link' when referring to history?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'primarily' correctly to indicate the main function of something?
options: The wall is primarily a heritage monument not a traffic barrier.
The wall is primarilly a heritage monument, not a traffic barrier.
The wall is primary a heritage monument, not a traffic barrier.
The wall is primarily a heritage monument, not a traffic barrier.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Primarily' is an adverb placed before the noun phrase it modifies, with a comma before 'not' to signal the contrast. No comma between 'monument' and 'not' makes the sentence harder to parse. 'Primarilly' is a spelling error. 'Primary' is an adjective — it must be placed before a noun, not used as an adverb.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Primary' is an adjective; you need the adverb form here. Check the comma placement for the contrast.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is there a historic structure in a city you know well that causes practical inconvenience — a narrow road, an old bridge, an awkward building? Do you think the inconvenience is worth tolerating? What would you lose if the structure were removed?
